name: Razorpay DB Pool Tuning — HikariCP Optimization
scenario: Razorpay's payment service goes down for 8 minutes when DB slows. Root cause: connectionTimeout=30s and pool too small. Configure HikariCP correctly and add pool monitoring.

core.description: application.yml: maximum-pool-size=(4×2)+1=10, minimum-idle=3, connection-timeout=3000, max-lifetime=1800000, keepalive-time=60000, pool-name=RazorpayPool.
HikariPoolHealthIndicator @Component('hikariPool'): returns DOWN with pending count detail if pending > 5, UP otherwise.
@SpringBootTest (3 tests): utilization gauge registered (0-1 range), health is UP, SELECT 1 executes successfully. All 3 pass.
README: explains the 8-minute outage root cause and how each config fix prevents it.

bonus.description: Multi-DataSource: payments (pool=10) + analytics (pool=20) with separate pool names. Test: both pool metrics available.
PgBouncer documentation: pgbouncer.ini config file + application.yml changes needed for transaction mode.
Pool exhaustion Grafana alert: PromQL for pending>5 AND acquire p99 > 1s. alertmanager routing to payments Slack channel.

ai_policy_explanation: 5 hints. connectionTimeout=3000ms: how long a Tomcat thread waits for a free connection from the pool. 30s (old default) = threads queue for 30s during DB pressure. 3s = fail fast, circuit breaker fires. max-lifetime must be less than PostgreSQL's wait_timeout (8 hours by default) — set to 30 minutes (1800000ms). keepalive-time: HikariCP sends keepalive query every N ms to prevent firewall/NAT from killing idle connections. Pool formula: (DB_server_cores × 2) + effective_spindle_count — counterintuitively small! hikaricp.connections.pending: the key metric for pool health.
